did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), a deep learning method, to classify skin lesions (melanoma vs. non-melanoma) based on texture features extracted via multi-scale decomposition. The CNN was used as the classifier to obtain results, achieving an accuracy of 93.50%.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents an original experimental study proposing a new method for skin lesion classification using a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with multi-scale decomposition. It includes a 'Proposed Approach' section detailing the methodology, an 'Experimentation' section describing the dataset (ISIC challenge, 1000 images), and reports original results (93.50% accuracy) compared against baseline methods. It is not a review or survey.

title: Texture Classification of skin lesion using convolutional neural network
brief_summary: This study proposes a deep learning method for classifying skin lesions as melanoma or non-melanoma using a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) that processes only texture features rather than whole images. The approach utilizes multi-scale decomposition via the Aujol Model to extract texture components from segmented regions of interest, thereby eliminating the need for manual feature selection. The model was evaluated on a dataset of 1000 images from the ISIC challenge, comprising 350 melanoma and 650 benign nevus cases, with 800 images used for training and 200 for testing. Experimental results demonstrated that the proposed CNN, which takes the Region of Interest projected with the texture component as input, achieved an accuracy of 93.50%. This performance surpassed other configurations, such as using only the object component (90.03%) or the ROI alone (86.21%), as well as a baseline method from literature (91.50%). The findings suggest that using texture information extracted through multi-scale decomposition significantly improves classification accuracy for skin cancer detection.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two classes in the ISIC challenge dataset used for experiments (350 images). Defined as a malignant tumor derived from melanocytes.
One of the two classes in the ISIC challenge dataset used for experiments (650 images). Defined as benign tumors. The study classifies lesions as either melanoma or non-melanoma (benign).

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into melanoma (malignant) or non-melanoma (benign nevus) categories.
all_task_types.details: The paper proposes a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) to classify skin lesions based on texture features. The task is explicitly defined as distinguishing between melanoma and non-melanoma (benign) using a dataset of 1000 images (350 melanoma, 650 benign). The model outputs class probabilities, and the class with the highest probability is chosen for the final diagnosis. Performance is measured using Accuracy, Sensitivity, and Specificity.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Challenge Dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dataset from the ISIC (International Skin Imaging Collaboration) challenge used for skin lesion classification experiments.

use.training: 800 images were used for the training process of the Convolutional Neural Network.
use.testing: 200 images were used for the test phase to evaluate classification accuracy.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using Accuracy, Sensitivity, and Specificity on a test set of 200 images, with comparisons made against baseline methods based on accuracy metrics alone.

ISIC Challenge Dataset.evaluation_setting: Binary classification of skin lesions (Melanoma vs. Non-Melanoma) using texture features extracted via multi-scale decomposition (Aujol model) and Otsu segmentation.
ISIC Challenge Dataset.notes: The proposed approach (CNN on texture ROI) achieved the highest accuracy. The paper notes that using only the object component resulted in 86% accuracy (Table 1 lists 86.21%), and using only the texture component resulted in 90.03% accuracy. The baseline method from reference [11] using Gabor filter and SVM achieved 77.65% accuracy. The baseline from reference [22] achieved 91.50% accuracy on the same dataset.

architecture_used.pretraining: Not reported (trained from scratch on the ISIC dataset)
architecture_used.input_size: Not explicitly reported (input is the Region of Interest projected with the texture component)

layers.description: Outputs of the four layers are flattened

training_details.hardware: Intel Core i7 processor, 8 GB RAM, NVIDIA GeForce GPU
training_details.loss_function: Not reported (Softmax activation implies categorical cross-entropy)

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Gray Level Co-Occurrence Matrix (GLCM) with SVM
preprocessing_techniques: Multi-scale decomposition using the Aujol model to separate the image into texture and object components
Noise reduction via Partial Differential Equations (PDE) decomposition
Image segmentation using Otsu's algorithm applied to the object component
Morphological filtering to refine segmentation results
Projection of the segmented Region of Interest (ROI) with the texture component to create the final input image
conclusions: The proposed method, which uses a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) trained on texture features extracted via multi-scale decomposition (Aujol model) and segmented via Otsu's algorithm, achieved a classification accuracy of 93.50% on the ISIC challenge dataset.

Using only the texture component of the skin lesion as input to the CNN significantly improves classification accuracy compared to using the object component (86.21%) or the Region of Interest (ROI) alone (90.03%).
The proposed CNN approach outperformed baseline methods from literature, including a GLCM with SVM classifier (77.65%) and a previous study by Nasr-Esfahani et al. (91.50%).

The study demonstrates that deep learning models can effectively learn relevant features from texture information alone, eliminating the need for manual feature selection often required in traditional texture analysis methods.

The approach addresses challenges in skin lesion classification such as noise, artifacts, and varying image conditions by employing pre-processing steps including PDE decomposition for noise reduction and morphological filtering for segmentation refinement.
important_remarks: The study is limited to a small, imbalanced dataset of only 1000 images (350 melanoma, 650 benign nevus) from the ISIC challenge, which may limit generalizability.
The dataset is imbalanced with nearly twice as many benign cases as malignant cases (650 vs 350), potentially biasing the model towards the majority class.
No external validation was performed; the model was evaluated solely on a single held-out test set from the same dataset used for training.
The paper does not report sensitivity, specificity, or confidence intervals, relying exclusively on accuracy (93.50%) for performance evaluation.
Key hyperparameters such as batch size, number of epochs, and learning rate are not reported, hindering reproducibility.
The CNN architecture is relatively simple (two convolutional layers with 32 filters) compared to modern deep learning standards, which may limit feature extraction capacity.
The preprocessing pipeline relies on Otsu segmentation and multi-scale decomposition (Aujol model), which may introduce errors if segmentation fails, directly impacting classification accuracy.
The study compares against baselines from literature rather than using a standardized benchmark split, making direct comparison with other recent works difficult.
The input to the CNN is a processed texture ROI rather than the raw image, which removes color and global context information that might be clinically relevant for diagnosis.
